Name: -1_to_and_the_for
Representative_Docs: ["Right now I am not impacted. I can pay our mortgage, but I worry about losing my job in a recession.  I also realize funds are limited for home repairs, etc. Also, I am concerned about the issues that particularly SFCC students and adjunct faculty are facing. It's obvious the reason that some of our students have left the college is because they had to leave Santa Fe and cannot afford to live here. They often move to Albuquerque or further away. Sometimes they can handle the commute, but it interferes with their ability to even have a part-time job. As gas prices rise, they will not want to commute great distances to finish their degrees. ", 'In our department we have food for the students to eat. Our college has put in a food pantry, and we have a meal program that people can donate money for meals for students that have food insecurity to be able to eat and it is anonymous. ', " Offer regular food service with discounted (affordable)and/or free meals. Loss of cental food services have left many feeling isolated and students leave campus as soon a class is over, since there is no longer a place to visit with friends and eat on a regular basis.  State would need to address these solutions: full coverage of tuition, fees and books to free up funding for living expenses. Housing needs to be addressed by the city and state. While dorms are not usually considered for community colleges, I think if SFCC could offer limited dormitories for students who are required to spend large amounts of time in person on campus such as nursing students. I also think it would be worth exploring if the nearby IAIA campus has any empty dorm rooms that could be made available to SFCC students, but we'd need to find funds to help students with those expenses. It would also be helpful if we had more emergency funds available to students who need to move, etc. We do offer some limited funds via the SFCC Foundation, but I don't think they are authorized to give the amounts that would be needed to pay first and last month's deposit before someone is evicted. Also, if the state could assist with offering covered or more affordable health insurance for employees it would free up money for food and housing expenses. In general, rental options are very expensive and limited. Santa Fe seniors are on the waiting list for housing assistance for years. Young people do not have access to any inexpensive housing options. Santa Fe is slowly becoming a community where the poor and middle class have no where to live. "]

Name: 2_pantry_food_campus_the
Representative_Docs:
['Have a food pantry on campus', "Food desert studies
Food trucks
Agriculture projects
By in security It's short lived it was because of a natural disaster and unaccounted for event. But the things I hear from my students give me nightmares AB one student on the Hopi reservation in 2021 who called me to tell me what the variety of fresh vegetables they had they went into a gas station which is the closest place to buy food by hours to where this person lives And they took a picture of what they showed me was really disgusting looking bell peppers and onions some garlic and some potatoes. That's all they had on the shelf. And I mean it wasn't even as much as what I would put in my refrigerator for my family of 4 for 2 or 3 days and that was all they had access to after traveling for hours. I'll never get over the things I heard during a pandemic from my students I'll never forget them telling me on Easten that they didn't have baby formula or diapers had to drive hours and hours it's kind of sneakout and go camp near grocery stores so that they could hit up all the grocery stores they could to get as much of the stuff that they could to bring it back to the reservation. I'll never forget when my student who had 4 kids of their own and the their sister commit suicide and so she's not getting all of her kids so instantly she has 7 kids and it's the pandemic and she couldn't get formula I mean 3 of the 7 kids were under 2. I only want to be concerned with this teaching people I don't have to be thinking about if they have diapers and far below food for their kids. That's why during 2020 I grew a lot of vegetables between the rows of my hip and I don't need those vegetables to local shelters homeless shelters and I trucked some out to the nation. The conditions of boys benefit or miss from a students there's food deserts there's inequity in food lake at a level that is unpalatable For any caring human being. That's why I think we should have food trucks and it should be fresh fruits and fresh vegetables and we should have agricultural projects where we teach people to grow fruits of vegetables. And to help farmers distribute their product and to have more diversity in what they have and give them places for their produce to be sold directly. ", 'Have a campus food pantry']

Name: 20_students_school_housing_emergency
Representative_Docs:
['They have made active strides to provide for students. With the limited funding they receive from the state, it is hard to serve students and staff what they need. With grant assistance from the state, we could help solve food insecurity in rural areas such as this.', 'Colleges and universities can help address food and housing insecurity in several ways. These can include providing emergency food and housing assistance, increasing access to affordable housing, providing free food pantries, and offering education and programs to help students better manage their finances. Additionally, schools can partner with outside organizations to provide students with additional resources, such as legal aid and mental health support.', 'I teach one class at a charter high school in the south valley. I take food there regularly because they don't "like" the food the school serves and always complain they are hungry, so they gladly take my packaged food which is primarily junk food. But I figure a treat is a treat, and if they know someone cares, that could mean a lot to them. The systems aren't working for everyone. I'm so lucky to be in such a place of privilege that I didn't earn. I lead a very privileged life compared to the students at the charter school. For example:
There was a shooting a few weeks ago. Two students were killed. Another student misses her dad who left her a decade ago to go to Mexico once he was "wanted" and has come back to the area but hasn't responded that he wants to see her. Another student lost a 2-year-old cousin about 2 weeks ago. Most all live in single parent homes. Apartments. South Valley houses with extended families sometimes. These kids have a very hard life, right here in Albuquerque where we ought to be able to do better. 
I share this because I can see the disparity and it breaks my heart. 
Thanks for initiating this survey to see what needs are out there.']

Name: 50_faculty_staff_salaries_raise
Representative_Docs: ['increase salaries for faculty and staff', 'Pay higher wages to staff. The fact that faculty get bonuses and incentives and annual raises every single year, whereas staff has only seen an increase in the last couple of years is absurd. Staff often work harder than faculty and without the perks of FOM and annual raises and bonuses. You cannot discuss equity without talking about the glaring issue that faculty earn so much more than staff, and many have simply completed a basic degree. ', "When inflation hit, my rent, groceries, and utilities started going on credit cards.  With inflation still above 6% and faculty's 2022 raise at 4%, I am losing income by continuing to work at SFCC.  I'm sure any raise in 2023 will also mimic last year, being under 5% for faculty, and administrators will receive at least 10%, so that any legal requirements imposed can be met, such as a 7% average across the institution."]

Name: 51_we_pay_insurance_earn
Representative_Docs:
["Northern needs to pay their staff more. We do so much in our office who is short staffed and we are not getting enough pay. My office as well as others in Student Services have been stressed and tired and some just don't think its is worth it to stay here, that is why we lose so many good collogues. We also should be able to receive a certain amount of mental health days to use to recuperate and destress. If we were paid more, we wouldn't have to worry about not paying our rent/mortgage or bills on time, which would cut down on stress therefore increasing better productivity in all offices ", 'The pay is insufficient even for faculty who have been here many years.  Students, who we encourage to get an education, see people with 10 + years of university and PhDs earning less than a manager at a Starbucks with a high school diploma.  They take note of this and basically think we are stupid to go into a profession that pays so little.  The college's view is that if anyone in teaching wants to earn good money, get out of teaching as fast as possible, and go into administration because that's where the money is.  But, what if teaching is our gift, and we want to share that, but also earn a decent salary--the college doesn't care, the Board and the Legislature doesn't have a clue, and they think administrators are more qualified (nothing could be further from the truth) or that they have more responsibility . . . but they are constantly saying that we, the faculty, are face-to-face with students and that's the most important thing, but obviously it isn't because they won't pay us what we're worth.  So many people are leaving this profession because of poor treatment.  NEVER say "people don't go into education for the money, it's a vocation" NO IT ISN'T, it's a job, a job that requires years of graduate school.  We earn and deserve decent pay--we're not Mother Theresa, we're people who want to look after our families. ', 'Be more open to diversity when hiring and paying employees and giving that extension to students who may become employees. I have applied to internal jobs for which I am qualified (or very slightly under qualified). There is a clear bias (gender, personality, ability to manipulate) in who is hired. 
Diversity when paying, I have more qualifications, experience, and oversee more projects than anyone however the degree is what determines the pay (I cannot afford to pursue a new degree even if it benefits the college). I can get work dones (when my mind is clear) 2-4 times faster, yet get payed per hour at the same rate. My tasks are complete and I get payed for 1 hour when someone else gets paid for 3 just cause it took them longer. 25% of my pay goes to taxes and more if I had insurance through them. Take home pay is 12 dollars and 13 dollars for many full time workers. 
I do not want free housing (I like my house I just need to afford it). We do not free food ( I see too much free food get thrown away. We need reasonable pay for living and buying what we need. When we pay for it, we endear it. (I am told the benefits outweigh the pay) the benefits are restricted and mean nothing if I cannot live in peace. 
As far as students, go if this continues, what hope do they have but to fall into the same net. They might have free food items today but do they know how to cook with what is free? Do they have the appliance, the cooking place, the utilities, the secure home? ']

", 'I have had the pleasure and enjoyed my work here at UNM especially with the students and staff. Unfortunately, I have had to make the hard decision to leave UNM to pursue other employment opportunities based on higher wages, less retirement deductions and no parking fees. UNM entity is a great establishment I am just so sad I have to leave based on my current needs financially. I am praying I can come back once all this financial hardship passes. I love the campus!  ', 'Contribute to existing efforts! The Dean of Students Office and LoboRESPECT Advocacy Center are already doing so much to help with food and housing insecurity! Name: 71_transportation_bus_route_protocol
Representative_Docs: ["I do not have such problems. However please consider whether organized commutes to & from El Paso or Hatch couldn't help students who live there but study at NMSU-Las Cruces. I regularly have students miss class because of mechanical issues on the road. There are buses between El Paso & Las Cruces, but it's worth asking whether options couldn't be improved either by working more closely with existing busing options or creating new ones.", "I appreciate that this survey exists. I think the public transportation piece is really important. We were excited about the Blue Bus, but haven't heard any updates. Is that still happening? It would be great if we could have a UNM-Taos shuttle that goes into town. It would be great advertising for UNM-Taos and could maybe even increase enrollment. ", 'The city of Albuquerque has decreased bus services; they discontinued a route that would place me within walking distance of my home. I now must ride later bus and walk in the dark home at lease 1-2 times a week. The commuter routes feel safer than the Lomas route (example) I have had two scary rides with people who had outbursts and were threatening to the bus driver and other passengers. the city bus is much more focused on down-town workers with regular work hours and not UNM workers who have later and earlier working hours that are not consistent. I  have only one car and share with my spouse so that I can afford for my disabled son to have transportation; I pay for the least expensive UNM parking, but park in the lot only about once a week. It is expensive parking for just once a week.\nUNMH does not have a program or set protocol for adult individuals that have traumatic brain injury. It is very difficult to get services and their is not a set protocol as there is in other states. This is a shame as the incidence of TBI is high and it is so very difficult to get SSDI for this condition. As the flagship University and hub for medical care in NM this it is a shame that we do not have a set protocol for caring with individuals who sustain TBI.']

Name: 72_insecurity_address_experiencing_insecurities
Representative_Docs: ['Many of the clients we serve have food and housing insecurity. This is very stressful as options are limited to address the issues they face long term. And not a lot is being done to address root causes. ', "Thank you for looking into this issue. It affects all members of out community directly and indirectly, even those like me who aren't currently experiencing food insecurity and housing uncertainty.", "Completing this survey revealed just how lucky I currently am to not be experiencing food or housing insecurity, I want to help those that are affected, but I'm not sure how to have an impact and help those in my community. It is horrible and shocking that people among us are experiencing these insecurities."]

Name: 73_tuition_totally_demand_fees
Representative_Docs: ['Students could very likely also use help with money management and budgeting -I have been shocked to see how much a student will spend at Starbucks but then say "i have no money" as an example.  Learning how to make better choices with the money that they have can make a big difference in their lives', 'Each state should provide a totally free on-demand/online option for earning a college degree.  The Khan Academy, for example, provides subject-matter lessons that are totally free, are available on demand, and are mostly far better at conveying useful information than in-class courses available at practically any of our public universities.', 'Again, colleges and universities have been corporatized to the point of becoming diploma mills that charge too much tuition/fees and suffer from cognitive dissonance in terms of students needs by superficially providing support but not LOVE. ']

Name: 75_healthy_harder_countries_rampant
Representative_Docs: ["It's disgusting that one of the richest countries in the world has students trying to get a good education and going hungry.  ", "It's getting harder and harder to afford healthy food. The meals at the schools are not very good but my kids are forced to eat it because there is nothing else.", 'I often wonder how students are able to learn anything when they are so hungry.  We have a real problem here in Farmington.  The fast food industry is rampant, but not much of a good choice with respect to the food.  I would like to see healthier choices that would keep our community from diabetes, heart disease, and cancer.']

Name: 76_dorms_dorm_taos_low
Representative_Docs: ["SJC has new dorms but I don't know much about them. My assumption would be to add more dorms. ", 'NMJC and State of NM could buy the vacant (Lea Regional Hospital) building near campus. Turn the property into low income housing or dorms, allow cafeteria access to residents. ', "Working with Taos County and Town of Taos, maybe some sort of low-income housing for students, teachers, health workers, etc.  I don't think a dorm situation would work for the Taos campus."]

Name: 77_move_commute_my_consistently
Representative_Docs: ['If the house I live in is not safe now, I will choose to move out to find another place, so it may cause my residence to be remote from my job location and affect my working hours.', "I lost my last job because they changed my work location, which I couldn't do with my car that couldn't handle the commute and was shared, getting two people to and from work. ", 'Consistently worried about being priced out of my apartment, do not take home enough after ERB and taxes to buy a home that is livable (acutally has wiring, roof not caving in, etc).  My apt building is falling down and owner makes no changes, when does do updates, raises rent.  I have a fridge that consistently stops cooling, so i lose my food.  I have an oven that jumps to 500degree randomly so I ruin/burn food.  These worries makes it hard to plan, prep, and save to find somewhere new.  All open listings of apartments are double or more what I pay now, so I cannot afford to move somewhere better or safer.  Do not want to move neighborhoods even if it is unsafe because if my car breaks I can still walk to work. ']
